generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components
instance MotorActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Servo motor assembly for dynamic control of MABI MAX 100 robot joints, providing torque amplification and positional accuracy"
]

instance GearboxActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision gearbox components for torque amplification and positional accuracy in MABI MAX 100 robot joints"
]

instance SiemensController : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Siemens SINUMERIK controller implementing real-time motion planning and execution for the MABI MAX 100 robot"
]

// C3: Physical sensing components
instance JointAngleSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Embedded joint angle sensors in MABI MAX 100 robot for positional feedback"
]

instance VelocitySensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Embedded velocity sensors in MABI MAX 100 robot joints for motion feedback"
]

instance TorqueSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Embedded torque sensors in MABI MAX 100 robot joints for force feedback"
]

instance TemperatureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Embedded temperature sensors in motor-gearbox assemblies for thermal monitoring"
]

instance FrictionMonitor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Specialized friction-induced wear detection sensors in motor-gearbox assemblies"
]

instance BasePositionSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Base position sensors for MABI MAX 100 robot for spatial orientation feedback"
]

// C4: Physical-to-virtual interaction
instance JointAngleData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom JointAngleSensor
]

instance VelocityData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom VelocitySensor
]

instance TorqueData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom TorqueSensor
]

instance TemperatureData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom TemperatureSensor
]

instance FrictionData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom FrictionMonitor
]

instance BasePositionData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom BasePositionSensor
]

instance ContextualDataTrace : DTDFVocab:Data [
    DTDFVocab:fromData JointAngleData, VelocityData, TorqueData, TemperatureData, FrictionData, BasePositionData
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance KinematicModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Comprehensive kinematic model of MABI MAX 100 robot structure and joint dynamics"
]

instance FrictionModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Physics-based friction dynamics model for motor-gearbox wear analysis"
]

instance PerformanceTrendModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Historical performance trend analysis model for cumulative friction loss tracking"
]

instance DigitalShadowRepository : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Passive repository for contextual data traces from physical MABI MAX 100 robot sensors"
    DTDFVocab:fromData ContextualDataTrace
]

instance OperationalProfileDatabase : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Baseline operational profile database for contextualized anomaly detection"
    DTDFVocab:fromData ContextualDataTrace
]

instance WearDegradationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Model for predicting friction-induced wear degradation in motor-gearbox assemblies"
]

// C11: Enablers
instance MontiArcAutomaton : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MontiArc Automaton framework for detailed behavioral modeling of robot components"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo KinematicModel, FrictionModel, WearDegradationModel
]

instance SysMLStateMachine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML state machines (v1.7) for behavioral modeling of robot operations"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo PerformanceTrendModel
]

instance MathWorksSimulink : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink for dynamic system analysis and simulation integration"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo FrictionModel, WearDegradationModel
]

instance MQTTGateway : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MQTT protocol gateway for time-series sensor data transmission"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo ContextualDataTrace
]

instance InfluxDB : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "InfluxDB for contextual data aggregation and time-series storage"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowRepository, OperationalProfileDatabase
]

instance RFLPFramework : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) framework for multi-abstraction modeling"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo KinematicModel, FrictionModel
]

instance SMArDTMethodology : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SMArDT methodology for structured Digital Twin development and evolution"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowRepository, PerformanceTrendModel
]

// C6: Services
instance MonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Real-time identification of friction-induced wear in motor-gearbox assemblies via sensor data aggregation and contextualized anomaly detection"
    DTDFVocab:provides FrictionWearInsight
]

instance VisualizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Dashboard interface presenting state metrics, performance trends, and alerts for MABI MAX 100 robot operations"
    DTDFVocab:provides StateMetricsDashboard, PerformanceTrendVisualization, CriticalThresholdAlerts
]

instance MaintenanceService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Predictive failure analysis for maintenance engineers using digital twin data"
    DTDFVocab:provides PredictiveFailureInsights
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance FrictionWearInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time identification of friction-induced wear patterns in motor-gearbox assemblies via deviation analysis from baseline operational profiles"
]

instance StateMetricsDashboard : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Visual representation of joint angles, velocity, torque, and base position metrics for real-time performance monitoring"
]

instance PerformanceTrendVisualization : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Cumulative friction loss trend visualization over operational cycles for predictive maintenance"
]

instance CriticalThresholdAlerts : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Alerts triggered when controller saturation or sensor drift exceeds predefined thresholds"
]

instance PredictiveFailureInsights : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Actionable insights for maintenance engineers to schedule preventive maintenance based on wear prediction models"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic false
]

instance MotionAccuracyImprovement : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Motion accuracy improvement recommendations derived from contextual data aggregation and analytics"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic false
]

instance RealTimePerformanceFeedback : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Real-time performance feedback to operators via dashboard visualization of operational metrics"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic false
]

// C17: Environment
instance RobotEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains MotorActuator, GearboxActuator, SiemensController,
    JointAngleSensor, VelocitySensor, TorqueSensor, TemperatureSensor,
    FrictionMonitor, BasePositionSensor, KinematicModel, FrictionModel
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "A Digital Twin system within this context is structured around a **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Kritzinger et al. [32]), where the **orchestration** relies on a **UML class diagram** to define key concepts for digital shadows in manufacturing. The model emphasizes a **one-way automated data flow** from physical objects to digital objects, where digital shadows act as passive entities collecting contextual data traces and aggregating them for specific purposes. The system does not explicitly detail temporal synchronization frequencies, multiplicities, or hierarchical coordination mechanisms, but the **active components** required to interpret and process incoming data (e.g., software systems) imply a need for real-time or near-real-time processing to maintain alignment between physical and digital states. The absence of explicit mention of time scales, granularity, or latency requirements suggests reliance on general-purpose data processing frameworks (e.g., tools like MathWorks Simulink or general-purpose languages like C++/Java) for handling temporal aspects, though no specific methodologies or frequencies are documented."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
    **Not in Document**: The provided documents do not explicitly describe the lifecycle stages of a Digital Twin (DT). While the context mentions modeling methodologies (e.g., MBSE, ADLs) and digital shadows, there is no detailed breakdown of phases like **conception, development, deployment, operation, maintenance, and retirement**, nor representation types (e.g., abstract models, simulation models, operational models) or transition management (e.g., how models evolve between stages). Lifecycle governance approaches (e.g., roles, tools, or frameworks) are also not addressed.

    **Implicit Context**:
    - **Development Phase**: MBSE frameworks (e.g., SysML, ADLs like AADL) and tools (e.g., Simulink, C++) are used to create **functional/logical architectures** and behavior models (e.g., state machines, automata).
    - **Integration**: Heterogeneous stakeholders’ perspectives are integrated via **modeling notations and tools**, suggesting a structured but unspecified lifecycle governance.
"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
    **Fidelity and validity** in this Digital Twin system are ensured through:
    - **Model validation/verification** via **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., **SysML, AADL**) and **simulation tools** (e.g., **Simulink**), which enable cross-tool consistency checks across abstraction layers (requirements → functional → logical → physical).
    - **Uncertainty quantification** is implicitly addressed by the **aggregation of contextual data traces** in digital shadows, though no explicit methods (e.g., Monte Carlo simulations, Bayesian inference) or error detection mechanisms (e.g., residual analysis) are detailed.
    - **Quality assurance** is supported by **stakeholder-integrated modeling** (e.g., via **RFLP, OOSEM**) to ensure alignment across heterogeneous perspectives, though no formal validation frameworks (e.g., ISO/IEC 25010) or uncertainty propagation models are specified.
  "
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
The **digital shadow’s technical connection** is defined as a **one-way, passive data flow** with no active control or feedback loops, as per Kritzinger et al. [32]. Key technical characteristics include:
- **Communication Protocol**: Relies on **MQTT** for lightweight, event-driven messaging, ensuring low-overhead transmission of sensor data.
- **Network Topology**: Implicitly assumes a **distributed, edge-centric architecture** where data is streamed directly from physical sensors to the digital shadow without intermediate processing layers (e.g., gateways) explicitly mentioned. The passive nature implies **no reliance on active middleware** (e.g., IoT gateways) for data aggregation or preprocessing.
- **Bandwidth/Latency**: While not quantified, the use of MQTT suggests **low-latency requirements** are met by the protocol’s publish-subscribe model, optimized for real-time, high-frequency data streams (e.g., sensor updates).
- **Reliability/Redundancy**: Not addressed in the documents; the digital shadow’s passivity implies **no built-in redundancy mechanisms** or failover protocols are specified.
- **Security Protocols**: Not detailed; the documents focus on the passive data flow without mentioning encryption (e.g., TLS), authentication, or access controls.
"
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
The provided documents do not address **hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise)**, **computational/storage requirements**, **scalability mechanisms**, **deployment models**, or **availability/reliability assurance** for Digital Shadows or Digital Twins. The focus is solely on:
- **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., SysML, ADLs) for system description, not deployment infrastructure.
- **No mention of cloud/edge/on-premise architectures, computational constraints, or redundancy mechanisms** is present.
"
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "The provided documents explicitly mention **standardization frameworks** relevant to Digital Twins:
  - **Industry Standards**: SysML (for modeling), AADL (for component-based architecture), and **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)** for Digital Twin interoperability.
  - **Compliance Frameworks**: The document does not detail certification requirements or broader compliance frameworks but highlights AAS as a critical standard for DT implementation.
  - **Interoperability Standards**: While OPC UA is not explicitly mentioned, the context implies reliance on **AAS** for standardized data exchange and model integration.
  No other DT-specific standards (e.g., OPC UA) are referenced, though AAS is a foundational enabler for compliance and interoperability."
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system incorporates **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** as a foundational framework for structured asset modeling and interoperability, enabling standardized data exchange and integration across the automation pyramid (as referenced in [42]). The system leverages **MQTT protocol** (as per [52]) for lightweight, efficient communication, though its specific implementation of message authentication/encryption is not detailed in the documents.

Physical safety is explicitly addressed through the **safety cell of the robotic arm (MABI Max-100)**, which restricts the operational workspace to a **cylindrical envelope with a maximum radius of 1.25 meters** and a **working range limited to ±90° azimuthal rotation** ([44]). This design ensures operator safety by confining motion to a predefined, non-interfering zone.

The system adheres to the **Reference Architecture Model Industrie 4.0 (RAMI 4.0)** ([49], [48]), which categorizes components across four layers (Perception, Network, Application, and Organization), facilitating structured cyber-physical integration. While RAMI 4.0 itself does not prescribe explicit security protocols, its framework supports compliance with industry standards for **risk assessment** (e.g., hazard analysis) and **fail-safe mechanisms** (e.g., emergency stop protocols) through its layered governance model.

No explicit mention of cybersecurity protocols (e.g., TLS for MQTT), access control mechanisms, or compliance with regulations (e.g., GDPR) is found in the documents. The provided details are limited to physical safety constraints and AAS-based data structuring."
]
